data.title: Two Quality Stocks Whose Price Declines May Be Long-Term Buying Opportunities
data.description:
Introduction

Typically, when stock prices pull back without any obvious reason, investors often wonder if there's something wrong with the business. Maybe the market is pricing in something we don't see.

Mr. Market is often forward-looking, so price declines before any news is very reasonable. However, in the case of these two companies, I think their price declines could be potential buying opportunities if you're a long-term investor.

In this article, I discuss two stocks and why investors should consider taking advantage of their recent price pullbacks.

Be Greedy When Others Are Fearful

Most of us are likely familiar with this quote from the Oracle of Omaha, Warren Buffett. But every dip may not be a great buying opportunity. Although when it comes to fundamentally sound businesses, it's prudent to take advantage of potential opportunities.

Sometimes the dip can keep on dipping, especially when buying cyclical companies that are experiencing financial difficulties. One example of this is Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA).

I bought the company for around $36 a share as I thought this was a great buying opportunity. Unfortunately, the stock price kept declining as their turnaround efforts weren't having any merit.

But what prompted me to sell my shares and downgrade them from a hold to a sell back in October of 2023 was their failure to raise the dividend, something they had done for years. The stock was not far from joining the prestigious list of Dividend Kings.

After not raising the dividend and giving a vague answer regarding the dividend policy, I figured a dividend cut was inevitable. Some readers argued with me that I was overthinking it and that the dividend was safe. Three months later, the operator cut its dividend by nearly 48%.

Over the past decade, WBA is down almost 88% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up roughly 193% over the same period. Walgreens has performed so poorly that it has agreed to a deal with Sycamore Partners to be taken private.

While the two companies I discuss today have been in the green over the long term, their prices have retreated in recent months.

Without further ado, let's get into these two potential opportunities.

#1 T-Mobile US (TMUS): Everything Verizon Should Be

In the chart below, you can see T-Mobile's price has pulled back from its 52-week high of over $276 a share. At the time of writing, the stock is down another $4 a share to $228.

And while some may say the stock was overvalued to begin with, I think many are confusing it with the typical telecom stock. A slow grower, high debt load plodder who typically sees 1%–2% growth a year. Basically Verizon (VZ).

I have nothing against Verizon, as I currently own shares and think they deserve a spot in every income-focused investor's portfolio. But how T-Mobile has been operating lately—they're everything I think Verizon should be.

Verizon was often known for its quality network. But they have been losing market share to T-Mobile. T-Mobile has not only been making acquisitions to grow the business but also raising its dividend while buying back shares.

Something TMUS did well was wait to start paying dividends so they could focus on the business. Verizon has been paying dividends for close to 20 years, while T-Mobile declared its first in 2023.

Since then, the telecom giant has conducted one increase, an impressive 35.4% (increase) last September. And for its upcoming increase later this year, I expect another impressive double-digit one, although likely not as large as last year.

They also announced a $50 billion buyback program at the same time, something I've criticized Verizon for doing in past articles. Through 2029, analysts expect double-digit earnings growth from TMUS. As a result, the stock is likely to see a strong upside, as stock prices typically follow earnings growth.

In its latest quarter, the telecom giant showed impressive double-digit growth year-over-year. EPS beat analysts' estimates, amounting to $2.58, up from $2.00 in the previous year's quarter.

Within its segments, they also saw double-digit growth, with the exception of Postpaid Phone Net Customers and Postpaid Net Accounts. Both declined from 523k and 218k, respectively, likely a result of higher for longer interest rates.

But both net income and free cash flow grew 24% and 31% from the prior year. Impressive considering the challenging economic backdrop. Adjusted EBITDA showed a solid 8% increase, allowing management to raise its EBITDA outlook to $33.2 billion–$33.7 billion for 2025.

But the main metric is that free cash flow is expected to grow this year as well. 2024's free cash flow amounted to $17 billion, and for 2025, management expects this in a range of $17.5 billion to $18 billion. For comparison, Verizon expects 2025's free cash flow to be between $17.5 billion and $18.5 billion, down from $19.8 billion in 2024.

As you can see, T-Mobile's free cash flow sufficiently covered its dividend, giving the company ample room for double-digit increases and share repurchases in the years ahead. In 2024, management repurchased approximately $11.1 billion worth of shares.

And these will also play a part in strong earnings growth. They've also been focusing on deleveraging the balance sheet with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.4x, down from 2.5x the previous year.

The company is expected to report Q2 earnings at the end of this month, and analysts expect earnings per share of $2.68, a $0.10 increase from Q1. I expect TMUS to deliver another beat with EPS in a range of $2.66 to $2.80 a share.

Recently, Bank of America (BAC) reinstated coverage on T-Mobile with a price target of $255, an 11.84% increase from the current price.

However, by the end of next year, I expect its stock price could be well over $300 a share if we manage to dodge a recession or market crash. So, if you're a long-term investor, T-Mobile's price pullback may be a good buying opportunity.

#2 Agree Realty (ADC): The Net Lease Costco

Agree Realty is another stock that has pulled back from a recent 52-week high. The REIT traded near $80 a share before pulling back to $71, where it currently trades. Moreover, with interest rates likely to be lower in the next year, Agree Realty's share price is likely to see upside as a result.

Although Agree Realty doesn't see the high growth of Costco (COST), its management team and execution remind me of a net lease Costco.

Particularly, ADC is attractive because of its long growth runway. At the end of Q1, the REIT had 2,977 properties, all based in the U.S.

So, they haven't even scratched the surface yet in terms of growth. Investments in Europe, the U.K., and other countries remain an option going forward.

But for now, I expect Agree Realty to continue executing on acquisitions in the continental United States. One indicator of its strong fundamentals and forward-thinking management team is its raised investment guidance for 2025. Management now expects acquisitions in between $1.3 billion and $1.5 billion for the year.

They also increased its AFFO guidance a penny to $4.27–$4.30, representing 3.5% growth from the prior year. One key advantage ADC has over peers, even big brother Realty Income (O), despite O's higher credit rating, is its minimal debt maturities and high exposure to investment-rated tenants.

Besides minimal debt maturing until 2028, ADC has been raising capital and has over $2 billion in liquidity in Q1. With virtually no debt for 3 years, this gives them plenty of flexibility to make acquisitions to continue growing the portfolio.

Leverage was also very conservative, with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.3x. And I expect management will operate within the 3.0x–4.0x range.

Going forward, I wouldn't be surprised to see them receive another upgrade to its credit rating in the next 2 to 3 years, which would also positively impact the share price as this would give them cost of capital advantages.

The most obvious reason Agree Realty should be considered is its monthly dividend. Last quarter, its AFFO payout ratio was 72%.

I typically look for quality REITs to operate in the 70%–75% range. Anything below 70% is even better. Over the past 10 years, Agree Realty's dividend has been well covered by its cash flows.

And I don't expect this to change anytime soon. AFFO grew to $1.06 while core FFO grew $0.02 to $1.04. The REIT is also reporting earnings at the end of this month, with analysts anticipating core FFO to retract a penny. I expect core FFO in a range of $1.04–$1.07 and AFFO between $1.05–$1.08 a share.

ADC also offers solid upside over the next two years. Furthermore, if interest rates are significantly lower than where they are now, I expect Agree Realty's price could move much higher, likely in the $80–$85 range at least. So, the pullback closer to $70–$71 a share may be as good as it gets.

Risks To Thesis

Both T-Mobile and Agree Realty could be impacted by a slowing economy. The telecom giant would likely see slowing net adds in its segments, while ADC could see impacts to its occupancy and slower investment activity.

Although recession fears seem to have dissipated in recent months, I don't think one is out of the question in the next 6 to 12 months. But as a result of its fundamentals, any further price drops should be viewed as opportunities.

Conclusion

Although both stocks still may seem relatively expensive, its strong fundamentals and execution in a challenging economic backdrop show its quality and that they warrant premiums in comparison to peers.

Moreover, both stand to see tailwinds from lower interest rates, which could also drive significant price appreciation in the next 6 to 12 months.

As a result, investors should view T-Mobile and Agree Realty's price pullbacks as potential long-term buying opportunities.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Circle K Owner Ends 7-Eleven Pursuit
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) pulls $47B takeover bid for 7-11 parent (OTCPK:SVNDY). (00:22) PBS, NPR face funding loss as Senate approves DOGE-backed budget cuts. (01:11) Wix.com (WIX) climbs after Barclays reveals hike on subscription plans. (02:11)

This is an abridged transcript.

Alimentation Couche-Tard has withdrawn its $47B proposal to buy Seven & i Holdings.

Canada’s convenience store giant (OTCPK:ANCTF) said Wednesday (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) that the 7-Eleven parent company had a "persistent lack of good faith engagement."

The surprise move marks the end of a year-long attempt by Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) who operates Circle K to dramatically expand its convenience store empire.

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) said it repeatedly sought a friendly dialogue with Seven & i's (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) founding family but that the Japanese company was not open to any conversation about the takeover proposal.

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) raised its offer to $47B in October 2024 from $38.5B, and in March offered to increase it further if Seven & i (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) cooperated and revealed more financial information.

Republicans are poised to achieve their long-standing goal of eliminating federal funding for public broadcasting.

The Senate approved a $9 billion package of cuts tied to the Department of Government Efficiency initiative.

The measure passed in a 51-48 vote, targeting the Public Broadcasting Service, National Public Radio, and various foreign aid programs.

Two of the Senate’s 53 Republicans, Lisa Murkowski of Alaska and Susan Collins of Maine, joined Democrats in voting against the legislation.

The bulk of the funds targeted — about $8 billion — was for foreign assistance programs. The remaining $1.1 billion was for the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, which funds PBS and NPR. The House is expected to give final approval to the package this week before sending it to President Trump for his signature.

According to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, Congress has fully funded it through Sept 30, 2027.

Wix.com (NASDAQ:WIX) finished the trading day on Wednesday 4.2% higher.

Barclays revealed that the website development platform had increased its subscription plans.

Barclays' price tracker identified increases on Wix's two and three-year plans ranging from 8 to 21%.

Barclays maintains its Overweight rating and $240 price target on Wix.com.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Is Mexican Coke coming to America?

AI bubble bigger than dot-com? Apollo economist sounds the alarm

Taiwan Semi rises after Q2 profit surges 60%, raises 2025 outlook

Catalyst watch:

  • It is the 70th anniversary of the Disneyland Resort in Anaheim, California (DIS). Disneyland is widely regarded as one of the most important and influential theme parks in history.

  • Merck (MRK) will host an investor event where scientific and commercial leaders will give an overview of the company’s advancing research pipeline in HIV treatment and prevention.

Stocks came off a volatile session on Wednesday, especially after reports of Trump's plans for Powell, but eventually ended in the green. Major averages also benefited from a softer-than-anticipated wholesale inflation report.

Trump said on Wednesday that it's “highly unlikely” he'll fire Powell “unless he has to leave for fraud.” Earlier reporting on the day indicated that the President would fire the Fed Chair soon.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is flat at $66/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.3% at $119,000. Gold is down 0.5% at $3,328.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.9%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) +31% — Shares surged after the company announced a strategic review that includes a 36% workforce reduction (~500 jobs) and a sharp refocus of its pipeline.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales

  • 10:00 am Business Inventories

  • 12:45 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in a moderated conversation before the 2025 Rocky Mounty Economic Summit.

  • 6:30 pm Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller will speak on the economic outlook before the Money Marketeers of New York University.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Missed The Circle Rally? Crypto Miner BitFuFu Might Offer The Next Big Opportunity
data.description:
If you feel like you've missed the hype around stablecoins, especially with all the attention on Circle (CRCL) (feel free to check out my previous story on Circle, where I laid out the potential and risks of the company), I will suggest looking at crypto mining companies. Personally, I am very optimistic about their future, and here's why.

I began investing in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) about five years ago, when the price was already around $20K per coin. Back then, I couldn't even afford a full Bitcoin. Since then, the price soared to $60K, dropped back to $20K, but look at the price right now. BTC just hit an all-time high at $123K on July 14, with people expecting the upcoming Crypto Week to bring new regulatory updates and major positive developments.

Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, trade tensions, and the volatile cycles of altcoins, I remain a firm believer in BTC and Ethereum (ETH-USD). 95% of BTC has been mined, only 5% left. Hence, the supply is limited, but the demand is growing dramatically. Bitcoin is gaining broader acceptance, not only among crypto enthusiasts like myself but also from major financial institutions.

Bloomberg reported that "Citadel is looking to become a liquidity provider for cryptocurrencies, betting President Donald Trump's embrace of the industry will usher in a boom for the asset class." More recent news showed that institutions like ETFs, big companies, and asset managers are actively buying BTC to diversify their investment portfolios. CNBC says, "Public companies acquired about 131,000 coins in the second quarter, growing their bitcoin balance 18%, according to data provider Bitcoin Treasuries. ETFs showed an 8% increase, or about 111,000 BTC, in the same period." Tom Lee projected that BTC can go above 150k by the end of the year, and the current price is not that far from this ambitious goal.

When the BTC price goes up, miners benefit. All miners hold large amounts of BTC themselves, so crypto mining stocks show a strong correlation to the underlying Bitcoin price. But BTC price is not the only factor/driver for mining companies' valuation. The two main metrics to evaluate crypto mining companies are hashrate (computational power) and power capacity.

A higher hashrate basically means the company has a better shot at winning new Bitcoin blocks, and more blocks mean more revenue. The way to get a higher hashrate is by upgrading to better and more efficient mining machines. For example, upgrading from an Antminer S9 to an S19 Pro could more than double the hashrate per machine. At the same time, increasing power capacity shows you the company has the infrastructure to keep scaling. That's huge, especially with energy costs climbing. Without that kind of backbone, it's tough to stay profitable long term.

Valuation Comparison

After checking out some of the major mining companies' recent operation reports, I summarized the comparison above, I feel BitFuFu (NASDAQ:FUFU) is significantly undervalued. I got all hashrate numbers from their respective June operation reports. This table shows that BitFuFu already has a higher hashrate than Riot (RIOT), and its power capacity is rapidly growing, soon approaching 1 GW. Yet, their market cap is only a fraction of Riot's, 1/7 to be more exact. Of course, BTC holding is the largest differentiator. Even considering Riot's larger Bitcoin holdings, this valuation gap still seems disproportionately large. If I subtract Bitcoin holdings from the market cap to focus purely on operational value comparison, Riot still stands around $2.2B in market cap compared to BitFuFu's approximately $377M.

To me, this gap in valuation makes BitFuFu a compelling investment opportunity that deserves attention. Here's my take on the valuation: If I were to value BitFuFu similarly to Riot based on their hash rate and operational scale, its market cap would be substantially higher.

As I calculated before, Riot's operational business (excluding Bitcoin holdings) is valued at roughly $2.2B, which means investors value each unit of Riot's mining power (each EH/s) at approximately $2.2B/35.5 EH/s = $62M/EH/s.

In terms of the cost of power, Riot's past operation reports listed $0.026/kWh to $0.03/kWh in different months, which is very similar to BitFuFu's $0.03/kWh. Normalizing for these energy costs and Riot's slightly lower expense cuts the premium multiple by roughly 10%. Applying the 10% reduction to the $62M/EH/s multiple yields an implied multiple of around $55.8M/EH/s, translating to an operational value of approximately $2.02B for BitFuFu.

After adding BitFuFu's existing Bitcoin holdings, the total valuation should reasonably reach about $2.24B. With BitFuFu's current market cap around $600M, this analysis suggests its share price could rise significantly to about $14 per share.

Of course, given that BitFuFu is headquartered in Singapore (unlike Riot, which is U.S.-based), is less widely recognized than industry leaders like Riot and Marathon (MARA), and has under half Riot's power capacity, I apply a 50% risk discount to its valuation. Therefore, a more conservative estimate for BitFuFu is about $1.01 billion, implying a share price near $7, still well above its current market level.

This quick, high-level valuation aligns with H.C. Wainwright's research report. According to the report, H.C. Wainwright issued a "Buy" rating and a $7 price target for BitFuFu. The analyst used a 3x market cap to sales ratio on a $383.5 million 2025 sales estimate for the $7 price target.

Cloud Mining Shifts Price Risk to Clients

One big differentiator between BitFuFu and Riot is that Riot is primarily a self-mining operation, as is MARA. But BitFuFu has a big cloud mining business. I got to know about BitFuFu because I was looking for cloud mining providers to expand my BTC inventory. According to its June report, the company mined "445 BTC in June 2025, an 11.3% month-over-month increase, including an 8.4% increase from cloud mining to 387 BTC and a 34.9% increase from self-mining to 58 BTC. Cloud Mining Users: 623,114 registered users as of June 30, 2025."

This shows the company's cloud mining business is scaling rapidly, and demand is huge, which aligns with what we discussed above: more company and institutional buyers. Unlike self-mining, cloud mining is a less risky business, because clients pay a fixed percentage of fees in exchange for mining returns, which helps stabilize BitFuFu's revenue even in volatile markets.

Backed by Bitmain

Another big reason I believe in this stock is that the company is backed by Bitmain. According to BitFuFu's prospectus, Bitmain is the founding investor and partner of BitFuFu. In fact, the founder of BitFuFU is a former Bitmain business director. Just to give some context, Bitmain is the dominant player in the Bitcoin mining hardware market, and MARA and RIOT use Bitmain machines too.

Because of the tight relationship, BitFuFU could have early access to the latest and most cost-efficient mining equipment. Newer mining rigs like Bitmain's latest Antminers typically consume less electricity per hash (measured as W/TH), significantly reducing operational costs.

Conclusion

There's no denying it anymore: BTC is entering the mainstream. In fact, research has shown that Bitcoin volatility is now smaller than the S&P 500 and Nasdaq indices. With strong backing from big financial institutions and President Trump, the tide is clearly turning in favor of digital assets. If you think the Bitcoin price is too high, definitely consider crypto mining stocks like BitFuFu. While the recent Bitcoin halving has squeezed miners a bit, slashing block rewards always does. This is not new. It's part of the natural rhythm of the industry. The players that will come out ahead are the ones that are built to last. BitFuFu, for example, has strong cash flow and is expanding its power capacity, backed by the largest mining equipment provider, Bitmain, putting it in a solid position to ride this out and come out stronger.

If BitFuFu can actually hit its ambitious goal of hitting 1 GW of mining capacity by the end of 2026, there's real potential for its valuation to jump. But of course, this is still a small-cap stock, which means higher risk. Volatility is part of the package. But the upside can be huge if things play out well.

data.title: Pricing, Tariffs, Social Inflation To Top U.S. P&C Earnings Agendas
data.description:
A changing pricing environment and renewed social inflation worries will be among the focal points for US property and casualty insurers when they release second quarter results.

Executives are expected to talk about the pace of pricing increases slowing after enjoying a historic year of underwriting profitability in 2024. Both the personal auto and homeowners segments booked year-over-year double-digit increases in direct premiums written last year as combined ratios eased.

Pricing and demand trends against the backdrop of tariff-driven inflationary pressures will be elements of the "big picture" for earnings calls, according to CFRA Research analyst Cathy Seifert.

"I think after a strong 2024, a lot of the tailwinds have gone," Seifert said in an interview. "We've got some headwinds and uncertainty, but we've also got a stable to acceptable pricing environment. We just don't have a whole lot of really strong tailwinds right now coming off a strong 2024."

An "overarching" issue is how tariffs and the possible "mass deportation" of migrants in the US impact the demand for insurance as well as "input costs, [such as] lumber, construction and auto parts," she added.

Earnings season kicks off in the morning of July 17 with The Travelers Cos. Inc. (TRV).

Ratios recover

An S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis found that sell-side analysts expect combined ratios to improve both sequentially and year-over-year for a majority of the 16 largest publicly traded US property and casualty and multiline insurers. Analysts predict that 10 companies will show improvement from the first quarter, which was overshadowed by the January wildfires in the Los Angeles area, while 11 are expected to show year-over-year improvement.

Four companies are expected to book ratios below 90%: Kinsale Capital Group Inc. (KNSL), Chubb (CB), AXIS Capital Holdings Ltd. (AXS), and The Progressive Corp. (PGR).

Assurant Inc. (AIZ) and Cincinnati Financial Corp. (CINF) are the only companies expected to post ratios above 100%, which would indicate underwriting losses. Assurant is the lone company in this analysis expected to see deterioration in its ratio both sequentially and year-over-year.

Catastrophe losses for the quarter appear to be "normal" based on April and May results published by Progressive and The Allstate Corp. (ALL), according to Piper Sandler analyst Paul Newsome. Progressive reported catastrophe losses of $206 million in April and $275 million in May, while Allstate reported $594 million and $777 million in losses, respectively.

Earnings bounce back

Sell-side analysts expect EPS to increase sequentially for 12 of the 17 companies in the analysis, while 13 will report higher earnings compared to the second quarter of 2024.

Companies expected to show both sequential and year-over-year improvements in EPS include Chubb, Travelers, Allstate, AIG (AIG), The Hartford Insurance Group Inc. (HIG), Kinsale Capital, Markel Group Inc. (MKL), Cincinnati Financial and Assurant. RLI Corp. (RLI) is the lone carrier expected to report annual and sequential decreases in EPS.

Progressive's revenues jump

Sell-siders anticipate revenues improving, with 14 of 17 expected to see higher revenues compared to the last quarter and 16 of 17 projected to see increases over the prior-year quarter.

Progressive should top the analysis in total revenues for the ninth straight quarter, with a consensus estimate of $21.70 billion. That would be a 19.70% year-over-year increase and a 6.32% improvement a quarter earlier.

Arch Capital Group Ltd. (ACGL) and Allstate are expected to report sequential decreases in revenue, while The Hartford is set to log decreases both sequentially and year-over-year.

Litigation worries back on radar

Social inflation, a topic that had faded into the background in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic, is front and center once again as litigation that was delayed by pandemic-related court closings is making its way back to the judicial system.

Chubb Ltd. CEO Evan Greenberg, one of the most vocal executives on the topic, sounded the alarm once again in a July 7 op-ed he co-wrote with John Doyle, CEO of Marsh McLennan Cos. Inc., for The Wall Street Journal, referring to litigation costs as "a hidden tax."

"The volume of litigation is growing rapidly," Greenberg and Doyle wrote. "Cases are becoming more expensive to resolve, and people and businesses everywhere are footing the bill. As the leaders of Chubb and Marsh McLennan, we see the real costs of this hidden tax every day through our customers."

Piper Sandler's Newsome said social inflation makes it imperative insurers keep a close eye on their casualty reserves.

"Companies differ greatly in their view of which products are most affected, which geographies are the most affected, the actual rate of social inflation, and the accident years most affected," Newsome wrote in a note to clients. "This suggests to us that, other than raising rates, the insurers have no long-term fix for the problem."

Investors shrugging off tariffs

Equity investors are moving away from defensive characteristics provided by P&C carriers' stocks in favor of those that are "more economically sensitive," according to UBS Global Research analyst Brian Meredith. The S&P Composite 1500 Property & Casualty Insurance Index, which fell 3.35% during the second quarter, has fallen an additional 4% since the start of the third quarter.

One of the reasons for the underperformance is that tariffs do not appear to be as much of a threat as they did at first, said Keefe, Bruyette and Woods analyst Meyer Shields.

"Everything melted down when it looked like tariffs would disrupt the economy," Shields said in an interview. "Broader investors have said, 'We don't think tariffs are going to be that disruptive,' and I think that's why a lot of things have recovered and the defensive appeal of property casualty insurance has faded in that context."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Staying The Course: Navigating A Volatile Q2 In Commodities
data.description:
Commodities remained resilient in Q2 2025; oil was volatile, gold rose, metals fell. Resource equities were mixed but showed strong fundamentals despite macro uncertainty.

Q2 2025 Commodities & Resource Equities: Resilience Through Uncertainty

Commodities and resource equities remained resilient in Q2 2025, navigating a complex macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop. Oil markets experienced pronounced volatility, driven by evolving OPEC+ supply dynamics and heightened geopolitical risk. Gold outperformed, reaffirming its status as a safe-haven asset amid global uncertainty. Industrial metals, however, came under pressure due to softer demand trends—particularly from China’s industrial sector.

Resource equities delivered a mixed performance, though the broader sector continued to reflect key investment strengths: robust operational execution, disciplined capital allocation, and an ongoing commitment to shareholder returns. While investor sentiment remained cautious—shaped by persistent macro and policy uncertainty—the underlying fundamentals and long-term value drivers in the space remain intact.

Sector Performance Recap

  • Oil & Gas – Oil markets experienced significant volatility in Q2. Crude prices fell to four-year lows in early May but rebounded sharply following the strikes against Iran’s nuclear infrastructure. These gains were short-lived, however, as the subsequent de-escalation redirected market focus to weakening global demand and OPEC+’s gradual rollback of voluntary production cuts. U.S. shale producers maintained a conservative approach to capital expenditures amid margin compression and elevated input costs driven by new tariffs. In contrast, U.S. refiners outperformed thanks to robust shareholder return strategies and firm capital discipline.
  • Base & Industrial Metals – Base metal prices delivered mixed results. Copper traded near multi-month highs, supported by ongoing supply constraints and relatively stable demand from China. U.S. exchange inventories fell to their lowest levels in nearly two years, driven by pre-tariff stockpiling and persistent mining disruptions. Supply remains tight due to project delays at major developments, while Chinese smelters face margin compression from historically low treatment and refining charges. In the iron ore market, resilient steel production and declining Chinese port inventories offered bullish signals, but broader macroeconomic concerns—including trade and property sector instability—kept prices under pressure.
  • Gold & Precious Metals – Gold prices remained robust, briefly dipping below $3,000/oz in April amid a risk-on rally before swiftly recovering. Gold equities generally outperformed the metal, supported by stable all-in sustaining costs (AISC) and strong free cash flow generation. Producers capitalized on favorable price conditions to strengthen balance sheets—reduce debt, enhance dividend payouts, and pursue strategic M&A activity. Recent consolidation trends have created opportunities for operational synergies and reserve replacement.
  • Renewables & Alternatives – The renewable energy sector continued to demonstrate modest growth in Q2 2025, though emerging policy and macroeconomic headwinds have raised new uncertainties. China remained the global leader, installing a record 60 GW of solar capacity, while the U.S. added 10 GW of battery storage in Q1 alone. However, proposed U.S. federal policy changes—such as potential reductions to production and investment tax credits, and the introduction of excise taxes on solar and wind—pose risks to project costs and development pipelines. Elevated interest rates and capital costs continue to challenge equipment manufacturers and independent power producers.
  • Agriculture/Paper & Forest Products – The agricultural sector presented a mixed outlook. Grain markets remained under pressure, with corn and soybean prices falling below breakeven levels for many U.S. farmers, despite generally favorable growing conditions. Margins continued to be compressed by rising input costs and ongoing trade policy uncertainty. Fertilizer prices surged, driven by supply disruption in the Middle East. Conversely, protein markets showed relative strength as tight supplies of cattle, hogs and chickens supported higher prices across beef, pork and poultry. Nonetheless, packaged food producers faced pressure from elevated production costs, tariff-induced input inflation, and subdued consumer demand.

Portfolio Performance: Drivers and Detractors

The Global Resources Fund (Class A; excluding fees and expenses, the “Fund”)(GHAAX) returned 5.47% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the S&P Global Natural Resources Index (the “Index”), which returned 3.26%. Year-to-date, the Fund has returned 13.34%, also ahead of the Index’s 10.42% return over the same period.

On an absolute basis, the top contributors to Fund performance were positions in Base & Industrial Metals—notably copper producers and diversified miners—as well as Gold & Precious Metals producers. Fertilizer companies within Agriculture also added to returns. Detractors included Oil & Gas and Paper & Forest positions, with integrated oil and gas producers accounting for the largest declines.

Relative to the Index, the Fund benefited from strong security selection within Base & Industrial Metals, an underweight allocation and effective selection in Oil & Gas, and an overweight in Renewables & Alternatives. However, performance was negatively impacted by selection and interaction effects in Agriculture.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%) Quarter End as of 06/30/25

VanEck Global Resources Fund: Class A

Notable Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, the team added to and exited some of its Base & Industrial Metals exposure, while also exiting positions in Agriculture.

Adds

Company Sector Weight Rationale
JSW Steel Base & Indus. Metals 0.76% One of India’s largest private steel producers, JSW posted strong results with EBITDA growth driven by rising volumes. We expect Indian steel capacity to double over the next decade, positioning JSW for long-term growth.
Cameco (CCJ) Base & Indus. Metals 0.83% As the world’s second-largest uranium producer, Cameco holds key downstream assets—such as refining and fuel manufacturing—where supply tightness is concentrated. These assets are a major valuation driver.
Antofagasta (OTC:ANFGF) Base & Indus. Metals 0.30% Chilean copper producer with four mines. Growth is expected from the Zaldivar mine. Barrick (2.07% of Fund assets) is considering selling its stake, potentially leading to strategic repositioning and optimization.

Exits

Company Sector Weight Rationale
US Steel (X) Base & Indus. Metals (exited) Exited following the Nippon Steel acquisition offer ($55/share) and perceived limited additional upside after meeting our price target.
Nucor (NUE) Base & Indus. Metals (exited) After benefiting from domestic policy tailwinds, we rotated out in favor of European opportunities, which now offer more compelling upside.
Ingredion (INGR) Agriculture (exited) Exited due to margin pressures from rising corn prices (+6% y/y) and potential demand headwinds from RFK’s proposed SNAP reforms impacting sugar-laden food sales.

From Policy Shifts to Price Signals: Key Market Drivers and Sector Impacts for the Second Half

The U.S. dollar is expected to remain under pressure, with downside driven by renewed tariff threats and growing concerns over fiscal sustainability. The passage of Trump’s "Big Beautiful Bill" could further widen budget deficits. Historically, a weaker dollar has supported higher commodity prices—a dynamic we expect to persist into the second half of 2025.

Geopolitical risk remains elevated, particularly in the Middle East. Although a ceasefire with Iran is currently in place, its long-term viability is uncertain. We believe a 5-10% risk premium is currently warranted for oil prices until a lasting resolution is achieved or market fundamentals regain control. Importantly, the distinction between oil at $60–$65 versus $55 is critical for high-cost producers and exploration-intensive firms. The current environment favors well-capitalized oil and gas companies with low break-even points, healthy balance sheets, robust profitability, and attractive shareholder return profiles via dividends and buybacks.

In the metals space, companies are benefiting from improved operational efficiency and resilient commodity prices. Meanwhile, volatility continues to plague the renewables sector as clarity around tax credits, other fiscal incentives and enforcement rules remain unclear. In agriculture, low crop prices should bolster the margins of protein players who will benefit from subdued animal feed costs. Conversely, subdued crop prices present challenges for fertilizers and agrochemical producers. Finally, the macroeconomic environment remains unfavorable for paper and forest products companies, where demand softness and cost pressures continue to weigh on performance.

Important Disclosure

All holdings and data are as of June 30, 2025.

During such time, the expense limitation is expected to continue until the Board of Trustees acts to discontinue all or a portion of such expense limitation.

S&P Global Natural Resources (SPGNRUN) Index includes 90 of the largest publicly-traded companies in natural resources and commodities businesses that meet specific investability requirements, offering investors diversified and investable equity exposure across 3 primary commodity-related sectors: agribusiness, energy, and metals & mining.

You can lose money by investing in the Fund. Any investment in the Fund should be part of an overall investment program, not a complete program. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Small- and medium-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks. data.title: Stitch Fix: Like But Don't Love Turnaround Story
data.description:
I reviewed Stitch Fix (SFIX) earlier this year and gave the company a “Hold” rating at that time. Although the company’s new CEO was making progress with his new initiatives, revenue continued to decline as the company’s customer base shrunk.

Since that article, the company’s stock has declined by roughly 5% while the S&P is up by over 5%. YTD Stitch Fix is flat.

While Stitch Fix has continued to make headway towards growing the business once again, not enough progress has been made for me to turn bullish on the company.

Let’s dig into the company’s latest quarter and see how Stitch Fix plans to continue reigniting growth.

With The Foundation Built, Growth Comes Next

In my last article, I noted Stitch Fix’s three-phase strategy, which was first rationalize then build and the last phase is growth. I would say the company has executed on the first two phases and is now entering phase three.

CEO Matt Baer stated the same on the earnings call, noting the considerable progress the company has made, “Over the last 2 years, we have worked through the first 2 phases of our strategy, rationalize and build, and fundamentally reshaped how we operate. We've strengthened the foundation of our business, incorporating retail best practices in strategic areas such as pricing, warehouse optimization and inventory management that enable us to operate more efficiently. We've also brought to life a more modern and dynamic Stitch Fix through our refreshed brand identity and the progress we have made reimagining our client experience. The improvements to our client experience includes 4 areas of focus: enhancing client engagement features, deepening client-stylists relationships, introducing increased flexibility to our service and offering a stronger assortment with more fresh and new styles.”

There are a few key strategies which I believe have led to the company finally returning to revenue growth. One of those strategies was letting customers have up to eight items in their fix. This tactic allows for more flexibility and gives the consumer more options which in turn could lead to a larger order for Stitch Fix. I believe it’s also giving the company’s internal stylists as well as their AI tools more data, as these insights can give the company additional information to help improve future fixes.

On the earnings call, management noted the company will begin letting first-time consumers have an eight-item fix so Stitch Fix can begin to accumulate more data for the user right from the first order. Additionally, the company is testing theme fixes, some examples of which include summer and workout fixes. Personally, I think those are great ideas as some customers may be looking to add variety to their wardrobe for summer vacations or when beginning to train for a new endeavor such as marathon or 10K.

Another new addition to Stitch Fix that has increased revenue is the addition of adjacent categories like shoes and jewelry. These new products seem to be successful thus far, especially footwear which has seen 35% growth year-over-year.

Overall, I think there is a lot to like with Stitch Fix’s offering and it’s creating a unique and unmatched value proposition within the apparel market. Consumers can quickly have clothes selected for them with minimal effort required, within their budget and with enough items to hopefully get their desired results.

Currently, it seems that consumer spending is holding steady as research from Deloitte suggests spending intentions remain consistent with 72% of respondents stating they’ve made at least one “splurge” purchase in May. Of these splurge purchases, coming in second is purchases related to clothing and accessories as you can see below:

With consumer spending holding steady, and Stitch Fix continuing to gain market share according to insights from Circana, the company seems to be on a path to steadily reignite growth.

AI is a Double-Edged Sword

I think one of the key reasons for Stitch Fix’s turnaround aside from the personalized stylists, flexible fixes and new product offerings, is the company’s implementation of artificial intelligence. Not only does it help Stitch Fix get insights for a specific consumer, but the company’s AI capabilities help the business with their overall merchandising strategy by making many of the buying decisions.

I imagine other retailers are likely using AI to help with similar buying decisions; however, I don’t think many are using AI to successfully shop for one specific consumer, or at least, not to the same degree as Stitch Fix. However, with reward programs and consumer data I could see more and more companies implementing personalized recommendations for consumers. For instance, if a runner continues to purchase the same socks and shoes from a specific company, I could easily see a customized email in which the company asks the consumer if another purchase is needed based on client history.

Now, I do think Stitch Fix has an edge considering they have a first mover advantage and thus many consumers look to their brand if they are looking to purchase something new or perhaps something out of his or her comfort zone. Yet, AI makes Stitch Fix's moat far more penetrable and the company with better algorithms and thus the better recommendations will likely win repeat customers.

Financials

In Q3 2025, Stitch Fix generated revenue of $325 million which is a slight increase (less than 1%) compared to Q3 2024. After numerous quarters in which revenue continued to decline compared to the prior year, it’s certainly positive that the company finally saw an increase as you can see below:

Stitch Fix now estimates they will earn between $298 to $303 million in the fourth quarter. The company has raised their full-year guidance as they now expect revenues of $1.254 to $1.259 billion for the full 2025 fiscal year. However, despite this increase, the company still expects revenues will be down year-over-year.

At the end of the Q3 2025, the company had 2,353,000 active clients which is a decrease of less than 1% quarter-over-quarter and a 10% decline compared to the prior year. Stitch Fix continues to lose active clients quarter after quarter as the graphic illustrates below:

However, on a positive note, revenue per active client is up and this figure has continued to rise over the last few quarters so the company is at least getting their loyal customers to spend more:

Compared to fiscal year 2024, the company has significantly reduced their selling, general, and administrative expenses, which has led to smaller operating losses and net losses as you can see for the three and nine month periods shown below:

The company’s gross margins were 44.2% for the quarter which is worse compared to the last few quarters as you can see below:

Management noted for the quarter gross profit margins fell due to lower product margins as the company sought more of variety of inventory.

Stitch Fix has maintained an excellent balance sheet with no long-term debt and enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities as you can see below:

Valuation

Stitch Fix has a valuation grade of a “A-” at Seeking Alpha:

Given Stitch Fix remains unprofitable, I think price-to-sales is the best metric valuation for this business. Stitch Fix does have a forward price-to-sales ratio which is better than the sector median and compared to peer The RealReal (REAL).

Compared to my prior article, Stitch Fix's forward price to sales ratio has come down compared earlier in the year as you can see below:

Despite Stitch Fix seeming more attractive from a forward price-to-sales perspective, future revenue growth doesn't appear promising over the next couple of years:

Comparability, the RealReal is expecting to see double-digit revenue growth by analysts, which is likely why Wall Street is more bullish on the RealReal compared to Stitch Fix.

Conclusion

Stitch Fix’s turnaround story continues as the company has finally generated positive revenue growth illustrating the company’s strategies which include, flexible fixes and continued variety, seem to be working.

Yet, the company continues to lose clients and remains unprofitable despite significant reductions in expenses. Also, while the company is using AI to improve the business, I could easily see a competitor mirroring Stitch Fix’s customized approach.

While the valuation seems attractive, the company’s outlook seems far from stellar. Until the company can make consistent progress in getting new clients and getting these clients to continue to spend more per fix, I remain on the sidelines.

data.title: EFA: A Flawed Hedge With Diminishing Long-Term Appeal
data.description:
The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA) appears as bland as it can get. One of the most popular international ETFs with an AUM of over $63b, EFA fails to impress by recent performance or hedging advantages. It tracks the MSCI EAFE index betting on developed markets ex-US and ex-Canada. Both the countries and stocks are selected on broadly a backward looking market cap criteria and currency effects are fully passed through.

Capturing Growth In The Developed World

As it turns out, the promise of growth outside the US from the developed world has not worked out in the past decade and more for long-term investors. However, the hedging and outperformance virtues become clearer when one looks at the last decade as an example. The MSCI EAFE index did outperform the S&P 500 for large sustained periods of time during the decade.

Hedging And Diversification

Directional hedging is still a drag i.e. don't expect EFA to bail your portfolio out when the US markets are crashing. Not at least for sustained large periods of time. The correlation has always reverted. The hedging benefits therefore be viewed from a outperformance lens alone. There could be periods where the US markets stagnate and EFA could be reasonably positive. Although equally possible is an underperformance from EFA. It is evident therefore that EFA is not a blind hedge. It may have diversification benefits over a very long term (spreading risks), but no sustained hedging benefit.

Diversification Continued

With most of the allocation concentrated in Europe and Japan, diversification is not truly met in the international context. Not as long as emerging markets are missing. Emerging markets do bring in added risks of political and policy uncertainties, but I am now talking from a risk diversification perspective alone.

In terms of growth prospects too, Japan is faced with an aging demographics and Europe with lower number of innovation/disruption focused mega caps that the US offers. Structural hurdles make the growth case no better than the US - as reflected in the past decade's returns.

Many US stocks now have sufficient global exposure anyway to ride diversified risks and growth stories - from Microsoft to Coca Cola. EFA with its conservative tilt towards low growth sectors like financials, still appears bland in comparison - all for international exposure that could be redundant. So yes, theoretical diversification is met in EFA, but it feels part growth stuck and part redundant - particularly in today's era.

Concentration risks are well handled and drawdowns could be more limited though. Within the EFA portfolio, the top 10 holdings contribute to only 12.75% of the overall portfolio. The portfolio is a large collection of 700+ stocks as well. So a few names crashing does not impact returns and idiosyncratic risks are extremely low.

Currency Impacts

The additional layer of currency gains if the dollar weakens is not a material impact in my opinion. For those who want to hedge the dollar against trade and fiscal deficits or easing rates, the impact on EFA is unlikely to cross single digit favorable alpha. A far lower worry to hedge against compared to equity risk and return profiles.

Yields Are Comforting But Alternatives Exist

For passive income oriented investors though EFA could make sense. A stable 2.5% -3% yield on an underlying with lower drawdown risks looks decent. However, drawdowns, although lower than say QQQ, are significant enough. In the 2022-23 rate hike cycle alone, EFA prices collapsed by ~30%. However, if yield is a criteria, there are other international alternatives, such as IDV.

Alternatives

In my view, the use case for EFA is limited, particularly in today's context. I do not see great growth alpha coming out of Europe and Japan in the next few years. There are far better alternatives if international diversification is the objective.

IPKW improves on EFA by applying a passive yet smart value filter of buyback intensity, capturing cash-rich firms likely to outperform in today’s late-cycle, valuation-conscious market. While EFA offers broad diversification, it feels growth stuck and thematically redundant. IPKW, in contrast, benefits from superior performance in macro conditions like now, where buybacks flourish. Its methodology avoids backward-looking screens and builds a more focused and quality-rich exposure across developed markets. And the outperformance is mostly across all regimes as well, although more so in today's context.

CGDG is another offbeat alternative. Although anchored in the US, it still has large international diversification and should play out well, combining US growth with risk diversification and good yields. CGDG also balances growth with defensives very well and provides a more favorable risk/reward profile for long-term investors.

For income seeking investors, IDVO offers a compelling case. While its overall performance is not far off from EFA, a yield of ~6% is great. And the diversification (including geographical) is far better than seen in the case of EFA - benefits yet to show up in its short tenure so far, but theoretically a stronger play.

Diminished Role In A Shifting Global Landscape

All in all, EFA appears to be an imperfect hedge to the US markets. Its growth prospects are not exciting compared to the US markets. And emerging markets are missing - which is by design, of course. I find some lesser-known names like CGDG, IDVO, and IPKW more favorably placed to meet EFA's objectives, i.e., 1) cushion drawdowns while providing a higher overall return and 2) provide risk diversification beyond the US.

There have been periods in the past where EFA has worked (such as the lost decade), but I don't see markets shaping up for a repeat given structural growth limits in Japan and Europe today compared to two decades ago. And even if such a regime change does happen, there is no hurry. Usually, if such a long-term trend emerges, it will span years, giving sufficient time for investors to switch gears when it happens and not in anticipation. EFA's effectiveness as a short-term hedge in crashes is not great anyway. Either it is missing or the hedge benefits sustain for a very short time before correlations revert.

I rate EFA as a hold. Even among the popular peer set, one can look at IEFA and VEA for lower costs with similar return profiles (with minor theoretical thematic differences and no real return profile differences). With geopolitical instability and tariff wars likely to cut both ways in terms of performance for EFA, investors could wait until more clarity emerges. In a de-globalized world of low correlations, EFA could make sense - if that happens.

data.title: United Airlines: I Am Lowering My Price Target After Earnings
data.description:
United Airlines (UAL) beat Wall Street expectations with its Q2 2025 earnings, but the stock showed little movement in after-hours trading. I believe persistent operational and macroeconomic headwinds may have damped investor enthusiasm despite the strong quarterly results. Since issuing a buy rating in January, the stock has declined 18.7%, underperforming the S&P 500’s 3.1% gain. In this report, I analyze the most Q2 earnings and reassess my buy rating and price target for United Airlines.

United Airlines Faces Pricing Pressure

The second quarter results showed that United Airlines was able to increase its passenger revenues by around 1%. However, this increase was realized through a 5.9% capacity expansion. Essentially, this means that revenues per seat-mile were down considerably.

In the domestic market, revenues declined by less than a percent. The 6.7% increase in passenger capacity was offset by a 7% decline in unit revenues. The Atlantic market, which consists of Europe and the Middle East/India/Africa, showed a 4.9% increase in capacity and a 2.5% increase in passenger revenues. The European market showed a 4.6% increase in capacity and a 2.4% increase in passenger revenues, while the other Atlantic market showed higher capacity growth, but similar to the European market, the capacity growth was partially offset by lower unit revenues. The Pacific market was the outline among all geographical markets, with growth in unit revenues as well as capacity. The Latin American market was the lowest growth market in terms of passenger revenues and expansion.

Overall, the second quarter results were primarily aimed at adding capacity to achieve revenue stability. Analysts had expected total revenues of $15.34 billion. United Airlines reported $100 million less, which I believe is attributable to trade and macroeconomic uncertainty, which are affecting basic economy revenues.

Q2 Earnings Show Significant Margin Pressure

Total operating revenues rose 1.7% to $15.24 billion. As we see in the breakdown of the operating revenues, we see that passenger revenues were the lowest growth part of the business. That is hardly encouraging. With capacity growth of nearly 6%, we see that neither revenue growth nor cost execution were extremely impressive. Revenue growth fell far below capacity growth on weaker unit revenues, and cost growth grew slightly ahead of the capacity growth. By excluding aircraft fuel, we get the non-fuel costs, which grew 12%. The increase in costs was driven by higher salaries and a $561 million charge in relation to a labor contract ratification bonus recognized in the second quarter. This was partially offset by gains on sales of assets. Operating income decreased 31.3% to $1.325 billion, indicating a margin decrease of 4.2 points to 8.7%. Even when adjusting for the charges, the profit drop was 9.8% to $1.772 billion, with margins dropping from 13.1% to 11.6%.

If unit revenues are not trending well and a company expands capacity nonetheless, they may do so to stabilize revenues, which seems to have been the case for United Airlines. Another reason to expand capacity is to reduce unit costs. The way United Airlines executed, unit costs increased by 0.6% and by 2.2%, excluding special items and fuel costs.

United Airlines Faces Uncertainty

The second quarter earnings definitely were not impressive. It seems to have been a quarter in which the company attempted to stabilize its revenues while market demand seems to be lower, as can be seen in the decline in unit revenues. Capacity additions are also not yet resulting in lower adjusted costs per seat. So, putting it together, we see a challenging operational environment.

That environment is challenging for several reasons. I believe the uncertainty on trade plays a major role, and the situation in the Middle East is also not good for business. Sentiment for investment in airline stocks knows major swings. Currently, with President Trump talking tariffs again and Israel attacking Damascus, that sentiment is not extremely great. United Airlines has been upbeat about the second half of the year, based on less geopolitical and macroeconomic uncertainty. I am not sure whether that view is the correct one. It is a very positive view on a very fragile sense of stability.

Lower Expectations Drive Lower Price Target

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For United Airlines, I have lowered the price target from $154 to $121.51. This reduction is driven by a 14% downward revision on EBITDA for this year and a compounded annual growth of 5.3% through 2027. Free cash flow growth has even been revised downward by 22.5% for this year, driven by the challenging operational environment with high costs and unit revenue pressure. The envisioned net debt-to-EBITDA ratio is now expected to be 2.2x compared to 1.5x driven by these downward revisions. So, there is a significant trim to the price target, but we also do note that the upside remains substantial at 37%. So, with the current expectations, I do believe that the stock remains compelling.

Conclusion: United Airlines Has Upside Despite Challenges

United Airlines is significantly more upbeat about an improvement in the macroeconomic and geopolitical stability for the second half of the year. Unfortunately, I do not share that optimistic view, and new threats from President Trump on tariffs and the attacks of Israel in Syria are testimony to significant destabilizing factors materializing or still in play. That, however, does not mean that I am walking away from my buy rating. The prior buy rating did not work out, and that is driven by the downward revision on EBITDA and free cash flow expectations for 2025, driven by the increased uncertainty.

For the full year, United Airlines is guiding for $9 to $11 in earnings per share, while its prior recession scenario assumed $7 to $9 in earnings per share. The prior stable economic guidance assumed $11.50 to $13.50 in earnings per share. So, the new guidance is somewhere in between that of the recession and a stable economic scenario, which in some way does show that significant uncertainty still exists.

data.title: Golden Entertainment: Downgrade Misses The Forest For The Trees
data.description:
Last month, I began covering Golden Entertainment (NASDAQ:GDEN), a small operator of casinos and taverns in Las Vegas, Laughlin, and Pahrump. You probably know it for its Stratosphere Las Vegas property (affectionately nicknamed The STRAT) and its incredible views of the Strip.

My thesis revolved around a few simple points: Since FY 2019, Golden has come a long way in cleaning up its balance sheet. Back then, we were looking at a Debt/EBITDA ratio close to 7x. But thanks to a mix of strong cash generation and smart moves—like selling Rocky Gap in Maryland, divesting its slot routes, and more recently the Nevada Distributed Gaming business—total debt has been trimmed down to $396 million. That brings the Debt/EBITDA ratio down to 2.9x (or 2.4x if you look at net debt), providing strong room for appreciation and double-digit growth in shareholder returns. Golden is also milking 5 to 7% annual buybacks, which combined with a 3.6% yield would give us a return of at least almost 9% annually.

Tack that onto the upside to my $32 price target, and we're looking at a total return of around 22–23% from where the stock was trading last month.

And do you know what happened?

We hit $32—and a lot quicker than I expected. My call from last month had us sitting on gains of just over 15%, compared to the S&P's 4.3%. Not bad, right? But then today it happened, and that total return got clipped down to just over 8%. Still ahead of the index, but the stock slipped back below the $30 mark.

What dragged the stock down today was a downgrade from Truist Securities—they moved it from a ‘Buy’ to a ‘Hold’.

Since we actually hit that $32 target, I'm not going to lose sleep over the price, but this update will seek to understand:

  1. The analyst's reasons for downgrading the stock and my opinion on this;
  2. More color on Q2 FY 2025 with new discretionary spending data;
  3. If some headwinds blow stronger, will dividends and buybacks be well covered?; and
  4. My rating for Golden Entertainment.

Truist Securities' Three Points to Hold

There are three reasons for the stock's downgrade. Let's discuss each of them.

The first is a weakness in the Strat, likely due to a less busy Strip this year. The analyst reduced his EBITDA expectations for Q2 FY 2025 by at least 5%.

Let's start with the second reason (since it has a lot to do with the first): Truist sees a weaker Strat not only in Q2, but throughout the rest of the year. In fact, Truist, which expected year-over-year EBITDA growth for FY 2025, now forecasts a 4% year-over-year decline (approximately $129.7 million). Note that these estimates are 2% below the market, which prices in stagnant growth given the softness of discretionary spending.

If you recall Q1, you'll notice that all operating segments saw year-over-year declines (Gaming, F&B, Rooms), so Truist sees this repeating for the rest of the year. Another factor they're probably not counting on is a sequential improvement in Gaming margins (as they actually saw, by 500 bp year-over-year in Q1).

The third and final reason is that M&A isn't expected to be a short-term driver for Golden Entertainment.

Well, I wasn't counting on that at all. In fact, I think they made this very clear in the Q1 FY 2025 earnings call, as I pointed out in my first piece.

For Truist, deleveraging could suggest a possible sale to some operators like Boyd Gaming (BYD), which has a much cleaner balance sheet after selling its 5% stake in FanDuel to Flutter (FLUT), or even the consolidation of other smaller operators.

The high valuation scenario would also hinder potential sale multiples and, consequently, shareholder premiums. They also argue that a multiple of 12.5x to 15x EBITDA wouldn't apply in practice here.

Making some estimates using Truist's guidance for FY 2025 (EBITDA of $129.7 million), we'd get an EV ranging from $1.62 to $1.94 billion. Reducing net debt from this, we'd get something like $1.17 to $1.5 billion. Note that in these models, the acquisition price for Golden Entertainment would be at least $44.30 per share.

A premium of at least 52% to where the stock is trading after today's slump.

Now, I don't believe in that acquisition price either. But consider this: if we use a target EV/EBITDA of 10x (slightly below the average for Consumer Discretionary stocks), we'd get a price of $32, right where my price target is, and a premium of at least 10%.

So, I still think this is a strong premium for the acquisition thesis, but to be honest, I wasn't even looking at it. I was, and still am, looking at the cash cow thesis.

At the end of the day, Truist maintained a price target of $34. That's $2 above my target and almost 17% of where the stock is trading today!

Honestly, this downgrade seems like a mistake to me. But a mistake for some is an opportunity for others in the stock market.

Let's move on to my next point.

Emptier Casinos in Q2?

I told you that despite a slowdown in Q1, there were no recession clouds looming for Boyd Gaming. I maintain that same position, and the data seems to bear that out.

First of all, Golden Entertainment issued an encouraging proxy for Q2: 6% year-over-year room occupancy growth at The Strat in May, strong performance at Laughlin, and Las Vegas Locals accelerating in April and May. Despite all this, PT's Taverns remain a strong headwind.

But beyond what we already had, what do we have on the table?

Looking at the GGR reported by the Nevada Gaming Control Board, we can say the following for each month of Q2:

  • April: Stability compared to March. GGR remained above $1 billion, demonstrating some resilience. Baccarat is still the underdog—unlike Macau—declining by double digits year over year. Slots were a strong point, growing 5.5% year over year in April, as was sports betting;
  • May: GGR was $1.29 billion, a 2% year-over-year drop. This was the fourth consecutive month since February that GGR fell year-over-year. Baccarat again fell by double digits, but this time slots remained stable while sports betting grew by double digits thanks to the Club World Cup. On the bottom line, visitor arrivals fell 6.5% year-over-year, while room rates are still trying to find a bottom;
  • June: We don't have specific data for this month yet, but if you asked me, I'd say it was also a decline. Restaurants and other discretionary spending experienced their worst month of the year aside from February, so I also expect traffic and spending to be down.

Note that this paints a very similar picture to Q1: slow traffic, falling by mid-single digits year over year, but with some tailwinds in slots and sports betting. So, while I think Q2 wasn't as good as the CEO painted it in the Q1 earnings call, it wasn't terrible.

Perhaps we'll even see some sort of sequential improvement.

Data from Placer.ai seems to corroborate this. In April, Nevada hotels saw a 2% year-over-year drop in traffic. This reversed in May (1.7% year-over-year growth), but returned in June (2% year-over-year decline).

Are Dividends and Buybacks Well Covered?

If dividends and buybacks are central to my 'Buy' thesis for Golden Entertainment, we need to understand if the Strip's headwinds become stronger, will they be well protected?

Starting with Truist's forecast scenario (EBITDA of $129.7 million for FY 2025). Assuming an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of approximately 80%, we would have an OCF of just over $103 million. With Capex of approximately $45 million, there would be FCF of approximately $59 million left over for the full year.

Note that even with the weakness implied by Truist's forecasts, this would be more than enough to cover the nearly $28 million returned from dividends in the TTM and still have $31 million left over.

Regarding buybacks, they still have $92 million in the current program. Assuming they don't take on debt, with the current program and projected FCF, they could repurchase at least 1 million shares (assuming a price of $30 per share) without seeing their cash flow deplete.

In this sense, they would reduce outstanding shares by almost 4% year over year!

Now imagine they use at least half of their cash on hand ($25 million) to accelerate this. In this hypothetical case, we would have $56 million available for these repurchases, still within the Truist scenario. This repurchase would be large, approximately 1.8 million shares, reducing outstanding shares by 6.8% year over year.

In both cases, adding dividends, shareholder returns would be at least 7%. I emphasize this because this 7% would be Truist's scenario without using cash and without additional debt.

Not bad for a return where you'd just have to sit and wait, perhaps on the beach!

Using my EBITDA projection from my last write-up ($132.7 million) and the same conversion rate for OCF, we would have an OCF of $106.1 million. Starting with a Capex of $45 million, we would have an FCF of $61.1 million.

Excluding dividends ($28 million), we would have an FCF net of dividends of $33.1 million. With an average buyback price of $30 per share, we would have 1.1 million shares repurchased, a 4.15% year-over-year reduction in shares outstanding.

A return to shareholders in the form of buybacks is still very attractive.

So, we can state the following:

  1. Dividends are well covered by FCF in all cases;
  2. Without using cash on hand and debt, Golden Entertainment could return at least 4% in buybacks (still a third of its total program of over $90 million). That is, this stock could generate at least 7% annual returns;
  3. I fully expect that with low debt levels, Golden Entertainment will generate strong returns from buybacks and dividends over the next three years. If EBITDA grows with a stronger Strip over the next two years, we can expect returns above 5% from buybacks alone.

My Verdict

Even $2 below my price target, I won't change my 'Buy' rating.

Think about it: from now until $32, we'd have an upside of over 6%. Adding at least 7% in buybacks and dividends, in the most conservative scenario, we'd have a total return of over 13%.

Even if Q2 comes in below expectations and within Truist's most pessimistic expectations, we'd still have a potentially strong return that justifies:

  1. The M&A thesis with a premium of at least 10%;
  2. The thesis of organic growth, milking FCF and distributing to shareholders;
  3. Even though they don't imagine this happening, the current debt level allows them to consolidate smaller players

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Contrarian Idea investment competition, which runs through July 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: ECB's 'Good Place' Threatened By Tariffs And A Stronger Euro
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

With the June decision to cut the main policy interest rate to 2%, the ECB thought it was in a "good place", hoping for some typical wait-and-see monetary policy over the coming months.

We had also expected a kind of ECB summer lull, secretly hoping to watch the July ECB press conference while relaxing with a summer cocktail in hand. Well, so much for wishful thinking. The reality looks different once again, and the ECB’s "good place" has become somewhat less comfortable recently due to the new tariff threat for Europe and the continued strengthening of the euro.

Next week's meeting, which initially looked like a summer breather, has become more exciting.

A stronger euro and new tariff threats bring more disinflationary risks

In recent months, the euro’s appreciation has dominated financial markets. Since the start of the year, the euro has climbed roughly 15% against the US dollar and about 7% in nominal effective exchange rate terms. Even since the ECB’s June meeting, the euro’s effective exchange rate has risen another 2%. Although there has been some levelling off recently, the stronger euro is now a clear concern for policymakers. While the overall impact may seem modest, current exchange rates - if sustained - would mean the ECB’s inflation forecasts for this year and next would be slightly lower than anticipated in June.

Historically, the ECB has been reticent about exchange rate movements, typically asserting that it has no explicit exchange rate target and considers the euro’s value as just one among many policy transmission channels. That’s why the recent flurry of comments from ECB officials about the stronger euro has been so surprising. Vice President Luis de Guindos even called levels above 1.20 USD/EUR "tricky and complicated." It’s worth remembering that it’s not a specific level, but the pace of appreciation, that usually worries the ECB.

In 2004, then-president Jean-Claude Trichet described a rapid move from 0.87 to 1.30 USD/EUR as "brutal." His successor, Mario Draghi, was less dramatic but still noted that exchange rate strength amid low inflation was a serious concern. Next week, it’s likely the ECB will take a cautious tone, downplaying the currency’s level but noting its potential to fuel disinflation.

There’s another complication: President Donald Trump’s latest threat of 30% tariffs on European goods. In June, the ECB’s baseline scenario assumed just a 10% tariff, and even the downside scenario only went up to 20%. A 30% tariff would be more damaging to the eurozone economy, even if European retaliation eventually pushed inflation higher.

ECB set to stay on hold next week, but not done with cuts - yet

All things considered, a wait-and-see approach remains the most probable course of action for the ECB next week. With the next potential tariff escalation not expected until August 1, there’s little reason for a pre-emptive rate cut now. The ECB’s policy is to act on measures only once they’re in effect, not when they’re merely announced. These days, it is a wise decision. At the same time, the strengthening of the euro since the last meeting has not been strong enough to justify a rate cut next week. This is why, despite higher uncertainty and probably only after a heated debate, we expect the ECB to stay on hold next week.

The stronger euro and the renewed tariff threats are clearly increasing disinflationary pressures for the eurozone, risking inflation undershooting and increasing the likelihood of more ECB rate cuts. However, given the longer-term risks of higher, not lower, inflation on the back of fiscal stimulus, the ECB will be very reluctant to opt for preemptive rate cuts at the current juncture. We maintain our forecast for another rate cut at the September meeting, though it seems President Christine Lagarde’s summer break may be shorter and less tranquil than originally hoped.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.
